headline: President Cleveland Signs Omnibus Statehood Bill, 1889
body:
Well now, gather 'round, folks, and let me spin you a yarn about the year of our Lord, eighteen hundred and eighty-nine, a time when the great, sprawling territory of Montana, a land as wild and untamed as a bucking bronco, finally got its spurs. It was on the twenty-second day of February, a day not unlike any other, save for the scratch of a pen in Washington D.C., that President Grover Cleveland, a man of considerable girth and, one presumes, even more considerable patience, put his name to a piece of legislation grandly titled the Omnibus Statehood Bill. Now, this wasn't just any old bill, mind you. This was the golden ticket, the very parchment that would hoist Montana, along with its neighbors North Dakota, South Dakota, and Washington, from the dusty plains of territorial status into the hallowed halls of statehood. It was, as they say, a turning point, though whether it was turning for better or worse, well, that's a matter for the philosophers and the old-timers to chew on.

For years, the good people of Montana had been hankering for a seat at the big table, tired of being treated like a distant cousin at the family reunion. They'd even tried their hand at drafting a constitution back in '84, a noble effort that, alas, was promptly ignored by the high-and-mighty Congress, much like a child's earnest drawing might be overlooked in a gallery of masterpieces. But this time, with Cleveland's signature, the path was clear. A new constitutional convention gathered in July of '89, a collection of earnest men, no doubt, hammering out the rules for this newfangled state. By October, the populace had given their hearty approval, and Joseph K. Toole, a man who'd already done his time as the territory's delegate, found himself elevated to the governor's chair, a position I imagine came with more headaches than accolades.

But hold on a minute, don't go thinking it was all smooth sailing and champagne toasts once the ink was dry. Oh no, sir. The moment Montana officially became the forty-first star on the flag, on November 8, 1889, the real fun began. The first legislative assembly, a veritable den of Democrats and Republicans, fell into a squabble over who would represent the new state in the U.S. Senate. Each side, naturally, wanted their own men, leading to a deadlock that would make a mule train in a blizzard look like a well-oiled machine. It took a decade, mind you, a full ten years of bickering and back-room deals, before that particular knot was untangled. It just goes to show, the more things change, the more they stay the same, especially when politicians are involved.

And then there was the matter of the state capital, a debate that raged hotter than a prairie fire in August. Helena, which had been the territorial seat since '75, found itself challenged by upstarts like Anaconda, a town built on the sweat and copper of Marcus Daly's empire. The delegates, unable to agree, punted the decision to the voters, a move that only prolonged the agony. In '92, no town could muster a majority, but by '94, Helena, with a little help from some rather substantial financial contributions, emerged victorious. W. A. Clark, another mining magnate with pockets as deep as a mine shaft, reportedly poured half a million dollars into Helena's cause, while Daly, not to be outdone, spent a cool two and a half million trying to make Anaconda the queen bee. It was a spectacle, I tell you, a true testament to the power of money and the stubbornness of men.

Finally, in 1895, a commission was appointed to pick a spot for the statehouse, a grand edifice to symbolize Montana's newfound dignity. Construction began a year later, a mile east of the legendary Last Chance Gulch, where gold had once flowed like water. And in January of 1902, the central portion of the capitol was completed, a monument to ambition and, perhaps, a touch of folly. W. A. Clark, ever the orator, was there at the dedication on July 4, 1902, no doubt waxing poetic about progress and prosperity. But I recall an old pioneer, Alvin Lee, a man who'd seen more than his share of Montana's wild youth, remarking with a sigh when statehood finally arrived, 'Now's she's gone to hell.' And perhaps, in his own way, he had a point, for with civilization comes a certain taming, a loss of that raw, untamed spirit that once defined the land.
